ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general milk tea entry; no specific brown sugar milk tea or less-sugar variant exists. Closest defensible parent class used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers tea-based beverages with milk; no brown sugar variant code exists. Closest defensible parent class used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for brown sugar milk tea or speciality tea beverages of this type.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard takeaway cup of brown sugar milk tea (less sugar) is approximately 350 ml / 350 g, representing a typical single-serve portion sold at Singaporean bubble tea outlets.

nutrient_source.title: Brown Sugar Milk Tea (Less Sugar) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id D08010002 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is set to -1 (data not available in source).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for sweetened milk tea / sugar-sweetened beverages
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for brown sugar milk tea (less sugar) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) by analogy with sweetened milk tea and sugar-sweetened beverages containing sucrose/brown sugar, which cluster at GI 60–70 in the Sydney database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international tables. Brown sugar is predominantly sucrose (GI ~65). The 'less sugar' formulation reduces sugar from ~12g/100g (standard) to 8.15g/100g, which lowers glycemic load but does not materially shift the GI of the beverage matrix. GL per serving = (65 × 12.39 × 3.50) / 100 = 28.4 g per 350 g cup.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 8.15 g sugar/100 g; added sugar independently elevates blood pressure via insulin-mediated sodium retention and sympathetic nervous system activation, irrespective of the low sodium content (20 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this beverage; the 'less sugar' designation does not eliminate the sugar-sweetened beverage risk, and the AHA recommends limiting added sugar to ≤25 g/day (women) and ≤36 g/day (men) — a 350 ml serving delivers approximately 28.5 g of sugar, exceeding the daily limit in a single drink.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 8.15 g sugar/100 g and negligible dietary fibre (0.41 g/100 g); liquid sugar is rapidly absorbed, causing acute postprandial blood glucose elevation with no fibre buffering.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should not consume this beverage; even the 'less sugar' variant remains a sugar-sweetened beverage that causes rapid glycaemic excursions, and genuinely unsweetened tea (0 g added sugar) is the only suitable alternative in this category.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar load (8.15 g/100 g; ~28.5 g per 350 ml serving) promotes hypertriglyceridaemia, endothelial dysfunction, and chronic low-grade inflammation — all established cardiovascular risk factors; non-dairy creamer component may contribute saturated and trans fat.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this beverage; the high added sugar content per serving exceeds recommended daily limits in a single drink, and the potential trans fat contribution from non-dairy creamer further elevates cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar content (8.15 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; non-dairy creamer may contribute trace trans fat which is a pro-inflammatory hard disqualifier for RA.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage and replace it with unsweetened green tea or genmaicha, which provide anti-inflammatory polyphenols without added sugar or trans fat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Brown Sugar Milk Tea (Less Sugar) is a freshly prepared Singaporean speciality tea beverage consisting of black tea blended with milk or non-dairy creamer and caramelised brown sugar syrup, served at a reduced sweetness level compared to the standard formulation. The drink is characterised by its rich, caramel-like sweetness, creamy mouthfeel, and dark amber colour from the brown sugar. Optional toppings such as tapioca pearls (boba) may be added. Per 100 g, the beverage provides 66 kcal, 0.69 g protein, 1.49 g fat, 12.39 g carbohydrate, 8.15 g sugar, 0.41 g dietary fibre, and 20 mg sodium, as reported by the Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo database. Despite the 'less sugar' label, this remains a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) with 8.15 g sugar per 100 g, delivering approximately 28.5 g of sugar in a standard 350 ml serving. It is clinically unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. The glycaemic index is estimated at 65 (medium) by analogy with sweetened milk tea and sucrose-based beverages, yielding a glycaemic load of approximately 28.4 per 350 ml serving — a high glycaemic load in a single drink. The beverage is popular across Singapore and is widely available at bubble tea chains. It is vegetarian and generally halal-certified but is not vegan due to the milk component.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database (cr_id D08010002, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). Exact match to the named food item. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021, clearly documented.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this freshly prepared brown sugar milk tea (less sugar) are not reported in the Singapore HPB Foodo database entry D08010002 as of 2026-04-16. No supplementary approved source (USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD) contains an exact match for this specific formulation. The input record's health_suitability section referenced a trans fat estimate of 0.1 g/100 g from non-dairy creamer; this is an unverified estimate and has not been adopted as a confirmed value — trans_fat_g is therefore set to -1 pending laboratory analysis or manufacturer disclosure. The dietary_tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' from the input cultural section are noted: sodium is 20 mg/100 g (low) and energy is 66 kcal/100 g (low per 100 g), but a 350 ml serving delivers 231 kcal and 28.5 g sugar, which is not low-calorie in practical consumption terms. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for this specific product is not available in any approved source.
